sentences.text: Yes.
Then, I had to take them out and put it on a dolly.
And then came in the next dolly.
I had to lift it, put it in.
And that went on and on.
It was very, very tiring.
It was very heavy to put it in, to take it out.
All of a sudden, one of these German guards-- you know, they were guarding us like-- I don't know from what.
What could we do?
But it's always guards were running around there with a machine gun.
One of these guards-- oh, I forgot to mention there was another woman, also a prisoner-- Hungarian Jewish woman-- who was supposed to put her foot on a pedal.
That's when the top of the machine came down to engrave the numbers in the shell.
Just when I was putting that shell into the stomach of that machine, that guard kicked that woman-- said [NON-ENGLISH].
I can hear it like it was yesterday-- meaning, fast-- faster, faster.
So she stepped on the pedal while my hand was still in.
If I didn't notice, I should have really lost everything up to my elbow, because my hand was like in it up to my elbow.
But I noticed it in time enough that it cut off my finger-- and-- and I have no nerve.
The little finger was cut off, and the ring finger, I have no nerves left.
But it was not cut off completely.
It was hanging like on a skin.
It was hanging down.
So of course, they didn't take me to a doctor or anything.
I just was-- of course, I couldn't work.
So I was put aside.
And I went back to camp with the rest in-- late in the evening.
So my sister went to the captain or whatever, that they have to take me to a doctor.
And I think she really arranged it.
I don't remember how or what, but they took me to a doctor.
And I went in, and there were at least 20 German soldiers sitting there waiting for the doctor.
And you know, one of them stood up.
And I could sit down.
Then, I see the doctor, and he's arrived.
And he cut it off.
Although, it-- I think it could have been saved.
But he cut off the finger and bandaged me up.
And his wife gave me an apple, which I hid in my robe.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They knew what was going on, and maybe they were more human than others.
I-- I don't mind saying that, because it proves the point that not everybody was like Hitler or Mengele.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyhow, I went back to camp.
And, of course, I was in terrible pain for quite a while.
It's a strange feeling, but the pain is up here, where there is no finger.
It's a phantom pain, they called it.
So after a few weeks, maybe a week, two weeks-- I don't remember-- they put me in the officers' dining room to clean the tables.
With one hand, I took the-- the dishes in the kitchen.
And of course, I really think that saved our lives, all three of us.
Because we were-- I somehow found a robe and put it around my waist.
So when I got back to the lager from the kitchen-- I had breasts like this.
Everything from the table, what was left on plates, just went in there.
And of course, I never-- I tell you now, I never had one bite of anything without sharing it with my sister and with my niece.
And that really helped us survive.
Not only that-- then, one of these German officer and his daughter took a liking to me, and I was to clean her room.
So I always found something there too, even a needle or thread, which was terrible important-- or a piece of bread.
Then, they put me in a place to peel potatoes.
And there was some kind of a iron stove, which wasn't used.
But we somehow found a way to make fire in it.
I don't know with what-- maybe with some potatoes.
I don't remember.
But I know we put the potato peels-- not the potatoes, because they may have been counted.
You know, they were shooting us back and forth if they found something wrong.
But the potato peels, we scorched them a little bit.
I really don't remember how it was.
But it was wonderful.
I went back to the lager.
I had potato peels up to here, you know?
I had always big breast when I went back.
When I went to work, [LAUGHS] I had breasts like a pancake.
And that reminds me that once, my niece was visiting me.
I think that my grandson was born, who is 18 now.
We lived in Rockford, Illinois.
And we went to Chicago to see the baby.
My son was married and living in Chicago.
And they took us out to dinner for a very fancy restaurant-- my son, and his wife, and my niece, and I, and my husband.
And my son looks at the menu, and he says, you have to take this appetizer.
It's barbecued potato peels, and it is the best.
And my niece and I, we looked at each other and started to cry.
We cried and we laughed at the same time.
Here we are in Chicago, and my son, he said, the potato peels.
I really felt potato peels saved our lives.
Potatoes saved our lives even later on.
And I tell you why.
It was so strange that he said that.
We talk about it with my niece.
Manita, I said, remember the potato peels in Chicago?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Anyhow, where was I?
In Lichtenau, working-- well, time went on, and I worked, you know.
And my niece and my sister, they worked in the factory.
And my niece worked two jobs there, taking care of my sister, who was very weak by that time.
She-- I used to wait for them at the gate when they were coming back.
And my niece's-- my sister's head was going like this.
She couldn't hold up her head anymore.
But she never worked in her life, really, and that work was very hard.
She worked with the shells too.
I don't remember what-- putting them on the dollies and lifting them-- putting up on the dollies or something like that.
But my niece tried always to be right behind her mother and do two jobs.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyhow, the time came.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember once I had to go to the toilet all night long, because it was terrible cold there in the winter.
And I had a very thin little blanket.
And I was worse-- I don't know why-- than everybody else with-- and I still have space-- some trace of that bladder trouble.
I went-- once, I counted.
I went 40 times in one night.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I was scared stiff, because many times I couldn't hold it to the latrines.
And I just had to go.
And if they would have seen me, they would say, you know, Jewish dirt, or whatever they called us.
[NON-ENGLISH]-- we were always called [NON-ENGLISH], which means men.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then, while I was there, I was also called a horse, because I and a very nice woman, who wasn't even Jewish-- her husband was Jewish, and he got there.
She got there, because she didn't want to be separated from her husband.
She wasn't Jewish.
We were the horses.
We had to pull a wagon up to a place where they had a warehouse and pull back the wagon with bags of sauerkraut, and potatoes, and salt, and what have you-- terrible heavy and hard.
And we pulled it.
Even pulling the wagon up on-- going up on a mountain was heavy.
Coming down it was-- coming down, if we-- should be easier.
But it was terrible heavy.

And that damn soldier [LAUGHS] who was guarding us with a gun, every once in a while made us stop-- which was wonderful-- and went to the barrel-- it was a barrel of sauerkraut-- and took out a big handful one or twice, and looks at us.
He says, this is really good.
This is very healthy.
But he never-- [LAUGHS] he never gave us anything.
He gave-- he told us how good it was.
Anyhow, once on the way back, we caught a soldier, a Canadian who was parachuting-- obviously-- and he was hiding.
I noticed him-- looking-- good-looking.
He are a redhead guy.
And he was-- I know he was hiding-- the way he was couping there behind the bush.
And that German noticed him, and of course, stopped him.
And got-- we got a prisoner.
So we were interviewed.
We were scared stiff, because who knows [LAUGHS] what they will say?
But I don't know what happened to him.
But it was the talk of the night that Magda and that woman, they caught a prisoner.
Anyhow, time went on.
And one night, when I was going to the toilet, I heard the Germans celebrating real loud-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --drinking and being loud.
I know they were drinking-- and singing.
And I heard something about Roosevelt.
I think that's the night when Roosevelt died, but I'm not 100%.
But there was something about Roosevelt.
And they were so loud.
And I know they were drunk.
And I never forget that one thing came to my mind-- that look at these people-- cruel, stupid, ignorant.
There is-- I remembered Emerson, who said that, 'There's only one thing important in life-- the starry eyes above me and the moral law in me.' That, in Hungarian, it sounds better.
And I'm sure the-- from English, it is different.
But that's how I translated it from Hungarian.
And that gave me a peace of mind.
Look at the drunken slobs.
But I am here, and I will survive.
Because I knew the war had-- oh, yeah, there were French prisoners.
They were not like us.
They were prison-- we were slaves.
These were prisoners-- men-- who also worked in a factory.
And they walked by our camp.
And every once in a while, they would try to get close to the point they whispered to us.
And we knew the war is getting to be over soon.
And when I heard that, I just had a feeling it won't last long.
I know, you know?
They were French, and I spoke French.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Only women.
Hundreds.
It-- I should know exactly, because it is in that German book exactly, and in my niece's book too.
But that guy who wrote a book about our camp says everything about it.
At this point, there were no more selections, though.
No more selections.
Then the war was-- the war was coming to close, and the Germans got scared.
And one day, we had-- didn't have-- nobody had to go to the factory.
So they put us to work in the vegetable yard.
I didn't tell you one thing-- how hungry we were, even there in Lichtenau-- that one night, I went out to try to some food-- to steal some food.
And I see the commandant's dog sleeping-- he had a big dog-- sleeping very peaceful in the front of a dog house, and a big plate full with food right at-- by-- at the dog's body.
And I had to do something, because we were very hungry, and there was meat on it.
And I know that's the end of me.
But I tried to steal that plate of food.
Because if the dog barks, that's it.
There were guards all over the place always.
But I did it.
And the dog didn't even wake up.
I took the whole plate.
We ate it like dogs, the three of us.
And then I had to go back and put it back.
What am I going to do with that plate?
So I put it back.
Nothing happened.
I tell you, God was watching over us.
But that was a wonderful stew with potatoes and meat.
But anyhow, the war was getting close to end, and the Germans got scared.
And then, one day, they said we have to go on a trip-- walk.
And then, we walked.
I don't know how long.
And we got to another camp.
Hessisch, it was called.
It-- I understood later it was close to Leipzig.
Actually, we were liberated close to Leipzig.
Anyhow, we got there, and by that time, I told you a German woman liked me, and I was her maid.
And those who worked for German officers, so to speak-- there were no officers, of course.
But that's how they had to be regarded.
They had to be put in a special barracks that they could find us easily.
So I got separated from my sister and my niece.
But I knew where they were.
So during the night, I got terribly scared that next morning, we will be taken away going someplace else, and I may be separated from them completely.
Because that was-- I don't know.
Not only us, but others were there too.
It was like a-- a congregation of all kinds of people coming from different lagers-- thousands of people.
So during the night, I escaped from that place and went to my sister and my niece.
And I stayed with them in the night.
And the Americans came with their fire bombs.
Nobody survived who was in the barracks where I was.
Next morning, we went and looked.
They were burned to death.
Not only that, they-- the-- that cot where I was-- another girl went in it, because, you know, there were two or three in a cot.
So when I left, she went there.
There was a-- an iron stood there, and that fell on her too.
She was crushed to death and burned.
That was a very big-- and the night was light from the fire bombs.
Of course, we didn't care.
We wanted the Americans to come.
We prayed for them to come.
If we die, we die, but they have to come.
So that's how I survived that night.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So we were taken away again.
But meanwhile, I stole 60 potatoes-- raw-- and three loaves of bread.
Because I had an access-- I had through the kitchen, or warehouse, or whatever you want to call it.
And I knew what's coming.
And I went back and forth, and back and forth, and hid all these things.
And my niece, and my sister, and I, we-- when we went again, we had 60 potatoes and three loaves of bread.
But I never felt richer, and never will feel richer in my life than I felt at that time.
Because a lot of them on the road just fell aside, dying from hunger and weakness.
But we were very smart, and we just had a slice of bread a day and a raw potato or two.
And it kept us alive.
Then, we had to stop again.
And we were told that we had to put into different groups.
And we had to walk again.
And they warned us.
Those who cannot-- who won't keep up, will be shot.
Well, but by that time, we knew that my sister will be never able to keep up.
We didn't know how, where to go, how long this will take.
And we decided to hide.
We still had potatoes.
And I think we still had a loaf of bread.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we went into a ambulatory, which was really stupid, because sick people were always shot.
But we didn't-- there was-- there were beds there-- cots.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And my sister and I-- my sister lied on there.
And Yutka, my niece, and I went under the sheet.
But we were so thin that it didn't show that we were under the sheet.
Because that woman who liked me so much, you know, who I was her maid, came hollering.
Magda, Magda, [NON-ENGLISH]?
Where are you?
Magda?
Magda-- screaming.
I didn't say a word.
We didn't move.
She could see my sister.
And she asked, where are they?
You'll never see them again if you don't tell me, you know?
And she was touching with her feet, our feet.
[LAUGHS] She didn't see me under the sheet.
Can you believe how thin I was?
[LAUGHS] So she left, and the Germans left.
And there we were, about nine of us-- us three, and there were six others who escape.
So now, everything is absolutely quiet.
Of course, we stayed there like statues.
We don't dare to move, because you never knew who would show up.
So I think the next day, we started to go.
And we started to walk.
We didn't know where are we going.
We had absolutely no way of-- no way of knowing where we were or where are we going.
And on the way, we met a German soldier with a gun.
And he asked us, who-- who you are?
What are you doing here?
And we said, we got lost.
We don't know.
And he left.
He could have shot us, but he left.
So we go, and we go, and we go.
And we see a big building.
So we try and unlock.
By that time, we had no potatoes and no bread.
And we were really sick from the raw potatoes too.
Anyhow, we went in.
And there was a Polish countess who was in charge of a camp of Polish prisoners-- women-- all women-- Polish prisoners.
They were not slaves like we were, but they were-- and most likely anti-German.
And they were there-- you know-- aggregated there.
I don't know if this is the right word or not.
So we asked her to let us stay.
We were terrible weak and tired.
That's all I need, she says, to have Jews here.
No way!
And we started to beg her.
She says, what am I going to say if they will look for you at the Germans' camp?
Well, she said, I'll tell you what.
Go down to the cellar, and stay put.
And never come out-- never a word.
And you stay there.
So we went to a cellar.
They were full with rats.
During the night, either I or my niece went and looked for food.
But luckily, we were not there too long.
All of a sudden, we hear commotion.
And we looked.
I lifted my niece up, because in a cellar, there was a little window.
She says, I think these are the Americans.
And they were the Americans.
And they liberated us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was-- they were so good to us that it made us terrible, terribly sick.
An American gave me a big, big pineapple juice and Hershey chocolate.
And we ate, and we drank, and we ate, and we drank, and we got terribly sick-- terribly sick.
But we survived.
[LAUGHS] We survived.
I don't, but it is in the book.
Because from that camp, the Americans took us to a camp with Greek women.
I don't know exactly where it was either-- not too far from Leipzig, you know.
Still we were sick.
We were throwing up, and we had diarrhea for two days, naturally--
--from all that pineapple juice and Hershey chocolate.
No, not at that point, no.
They took us to that camp.
And we were there-- not a camp.
It was an old hotel where they put us with the Greek women.
And we were hungry again, because there were a lot.
They didn't have too much food.
I don't know.
But they ate as much as they could.
And they gave us maybe a slice of salami and a slice of bread for a whole day.
We were very hungry.
And one day, I said, look.
It's-- we have one more slice of salami, and we are starving.
There are Americans some place around here.
And we heard there is a headquarters.
I said, we have to go and see where they are and try to get back to Hungary.
What are we doing here?
And we went to the Americans.
And we found out where the headquarters was-- American headquarters.
And we went there.
Well, there were at least five, six blocks of line already.
But we stood in line.
And finally, we got in, maybe two or three hours later.
We were ready to faint.
And we went in there, and these two nice-- we thought two officers-- but only one was an officer-- came to us and asked who we are.
And we said, we are Hungarian Jewesses, and we want to get back to Hungary.
And we talked a little bit.
And they told us which door to go in.
But this one officer, whom we talked-- who we met at that time, is my niece's husband.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Anyhow, but they said, you have to wait.
There is no transportation yet.
We will let you know.
But they put us-- they told us where other Hungarian Jews-- Jewish women are.
Meanwhile, an American gave a bicycle to my niece-- insisted.
And she didn't want to take it, 'cause she-- we were afraid, even from American.
What does he want?
What does he want in return?
He said, don't worry.
I don't want.
I am going-- I was placed to go someplace else.
There is still a war going on someplace else, and if you don't take it, a German will take it.
So my niece took the bike.
And he-- she found out where that camp is with the Hungarian women.
And we went there.
So--
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
